article.title: Overview of Routing Appliances - Azure Virtual Network

posts.text:
Virtual Network Routing Appliance ("Routing Appliance") Public Preview!

During Ignite, I posted about exciting developments in building a high-performance, native appliance in Azure that will help customers achieve low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need of a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). Today I'm happy to announce that we have released a public preview of this feature. I hope you get a chance to try it out and we are looking to build many more exciting capabilities on top of this new technology.

NVAs have been the backbone of complex Azure networking topologies where customers needed to route traffic through a central point. While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point.  NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps.  You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

Read More: https://lnkd.in/gWqdVEjv

Thanks to Ashish Bhargava, Mario Lopez, Mohamed Hassan, Jackson Crewe, Nitin Goel, Pan Wang, Anshu Verma, Arjun Patel, Rishabh Tewari, Neeraj Motwani, Avijit Gupta, Damini Singal, Michal Zygmunt, Deepak Bansal

article.title: GigaTIME AI tool advances cancer research and could unlock access to better treatment | Microsoft Signal Blog | Microsoft

resharedPost.text:
Cancer researchers often wait on complex lab workflows, so the idea that routine pathology slides can surface much deeper insight marks a meaningful shift. GigaTIME, an open-source AI tool from Microsoft Research, creates a detailed digital map that shows how immune cells and tumors interact, giving researchers a view that can help them explore treatment pathways with greater precision. 
 
Built on data approved for research use from more than 51 hospitals and 1,000 clinics, GigaTIME enables population-scale modeling at an unprecedented scale and empowers scientists to unlock new insights faster and more affordably. A joint collaborative project with Providence and University of Washington, this work continues to push cancer research forward. https://msft.it/6009t9lXF

comments.text:
Using routine pathology slides as fuel for spatial proteomics is exactly the kind of constraint-respecting innovation medicine needs.
The real unlock is wiring GigaTIME into trial design and standard of care with receipts, so every treatment change can be traced back to the exact slide, model version, and simulation that justified it. That's how “AI in cancer” becomes something a tumor board and a regulator can both sign off on.
Exciting work, cheering you all on to even more success!

posts.text:
I was cleaning out old papers this weekend and ran across my first offer letter out of college. 

Today we see expectations of signing bonuses that would exceed the annual salary I was offered and stock grants that I could never have dreamed of.  My signing bonus was $2,000. And 3,500 stock options. For those of you that are too young to remember what options were, it was the option to buy the stock at a given price. That was the strike price which was usually set the stock price on your start date. If the company had a share price of $10 when I started and 3 years later it's share price have gone to $20, I have the option to buy up to 3,500 shares at $10. That would have been a total profit of $35,000. Not bad but consider the fact that from day one of joining this company their share price never exceeded my strike price. So if I actually never saw a dime from my shares in this company.

To me it is a moment to remind everyone to be humble of what you have and how lucky you are to work in the industry you do. I chose this career out of a passion for computers and technology. I worry about the entitlement I see is starting to grow in this industry. Expectations of huge compensation packages without skill behind them. 

When I started at Metawave, I was hungry to learn every piece of technology I could. I worked in embedded systems and wrote c++ code to drive custom hardware. 64K of RAM and embedded microprocessor. I looked for the smartest guys in the company and spent every moment I could learning from them. 

if I can do another 20 years in this career my life will be fulfilled.

comments.text:
I feel blessed being in this industry considering how crazy the job market has been in the past few years. The startups I've joined in the past also had modest stock options, but unfortunately didn't yield anything after their acquisitions (not enough to cover the common stocks I had).

In retrospect, I'm grateful for the experience, connections, and opportunity for continuous learning from people smarter than me that keeps  me going.

article.title: What is a network security perimeter? - Azure Private Link

posts.text:
As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements.  This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space.  While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together.  It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound.  

Features of a network security perimeter include:  
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations.  
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter.  
* Access logs for audit and compliance.  
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: https://lnkd.in/gY-CvgY6

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:
https://lnkd.in/gJW6mwc4

posts.text:
Today I have two exciting announcements.  Let's get into the first one!

We are excited to announce that a public preview of supporting up 5,000 virtual networks in an Azure Virtual Network Manager mesh connectivity. A mesh topology creates bi‑directional connectivity between every virtual network in a mesh connectivity configuration, so selected Virtual networks communicate directly, eliminating manual peerings, avoiding extra hops, and delivering low‑latency traffic flows under a unified control plane.

The original limit was 250 virtual networks in a mesh with a maximum of 1000 with a support request.  The enablement to 5,000 will allow a huge increase in scale for our customers!

🎉Azure Virtual Network Manager's IP address management (IPAM) feature is now Generally Available!🎉

Managing IP addresses at scale natively in Azure has arrived. Whether your network environment is modest or massive, AVNM’s IPAM streamlines complex and manual processes into a centralized, automated tool. Here's what IPAM brings to your network operations:

✅ Centralized IP Planning -- create hierarchical pools with up to 7 layers for organized address space management.
✅ Automatic CIDR Allocation -- IPAM automatically assigns non-overlapping IP addresses from your pools to your Azure resources.
✅ Overlapping Address Space Prevention -- reserve IPs for specific demands to seamlessly avoid address space conflicts across Azure, on-premises, and multi-cloud environments.
✅ Enforce Nonoverlapping Address Spaces with Azure Policy -- ensure virtual networks use designated pools, preventing unintended overlaps for new and updated virtual networks.
✅ Pool Permission Delegation -- authorize other users and teams with controlled access to specific IP pools to avoid overlaps.
✅ Enhanced Visibility -- monitor IP usage and allocation statistics across your network resources.
✅ Flexible Management -- IPAM can be utilized across tenants to allocate available pool address space to virtual networks in managed tenants. IPAM pools can also be deployed with Bicep templates.

article.title: Pricing - Azure Virtual Network Manager| Microsoft Azure

posts.text:
One of the most asked for features by customers for Azure Virtual Network Manager (AvNM) isn't a feature at all! It was to change our billing model. 

Previously, AvNM billed based on subscriptions with managed virtual networks. However, this model didn't align with Azure best practices that recommend having a larger number of subscriptions. For many customers, this blocked adoption.

Now, we bill directly based on the virtual networks managed. This model of billing aligns with the actual resources under management and gives a more accurate bill for our customers. 

I'm pleased to announce that all Azure public regions now have this new billing model and all future network managers will be billed based on virtual networks.

Aidan Finn, I know you have been a big champion of this and special thanks for your ongoing support and feedback!

resharedPost.text:
Today we unveil the public preview of Network Security Perimeter, a product I’ve helped build since 2020. Kudos to the team for reaching this milestone and thanks to partners across Microsoft for their effort and support!

Here's an overview:

Azure allows you to set up PaaS resources like SQL servers, Cosmos DB accounts, Storage accounts, and Key Vaults to run parts of your application workloads on our cloud infrastructure. Currently, these resources offer isolation and some access controls, such as:

- Blocking inbound public network access via the publicNetworkAccess setting and firewall rules.
- Using private endpoints to secure communication with virtual machines.

However, until now, Azure hasn't provided comprehensive controls for locking down traffic to and from these PaaS resources:

- No granular service-to-service traffic controls: Since PaaS resources share Azure's network, you can't isolate traffic by IP or other network identifiers. Existing trusted service access support lets you selectively enable access from specific Azure services, but resource-level granularity has been missing.
- No outbound traffic controls: Many PaaS resources can send data to the internet, making it possible for a malicious actor with enough permissions to exfiltrate sensitive information by making API requests to the resource. Existing controls like setting publicNetworkAccess to Disabled won't actually block data egress.
- Disparate configuration and monitoring interfaces: How you configure and monitor these resources varies by service, which starts to become confusing and error-prone with only a few resources.

Enter Network Security Perimeters, which let you create logical boundaries, define standard access rules, and configure access monitoring across multiple PaaS resources in a single interface. Key features include:

- Default isolation: By default, resources within a perimeter can only communicate with each other.
- Standardized access rules: Within a perimeter, profiles define access rules for selective communication with external sources and destinations. When you add a resource to a perimeter and select a profile, the resource inherits all the access rules of that profile, eliminating the need to duplicate firewall rules across different interfaces.
- Unified diagnostics: Adding diagnostic settings to a perimeter provides a consistent experience to configure and analyze access monitoring across all associated PaaS resources.

To learn more about how to use Network Security Perimeter to secure your Azure PaaS resources, read the announcement linked in Chaithanya's post below.

posts.text:
Today is a great day for managing your routing at scale in Azure!  I'm happy to announce that my team is delivering the capability to manage your user-defined route tables at scale.

One of the complexities customers face with route tables in Azure is the management of linking them to multiple subnets and then managing the routing rules at a larger scale.  With route management functionality coming into Azure Virtual Network Manager, customers can now simply create a single definition and apply it to hundreds of subnets with just a few clicks.

Additionally customers have asked for the capability to have more than the 400 rule limit that exists today.  Customers who use AVNM for their route management will be able to create up to 1,000 rules in their route tables. This gives our customers the ability to create more complex and detailed route tables.

Great work by my team and I look forward to hearing how this feature helps your organization.

☁️ Azure Virtual Network Manager (AVNM) Fridays: Security ☁️ 

I’m excited to dive into a series on a fantastic product I’ve been working on – Azure Virtual Network Manager. Every week, I’ll share a piece of AVNM’s magic – this time, we’ll go into security admin rules! 

Check out last week’s AVNM Fridays post covering the basics: https://lnkd.in/g3qUWVty

🤔 What are AVNM security admin rules? 

Security admin rules are an AVNM offering that protects your virtual networks at scale. You can deploy a security admin configuration with several rule collections, each of which can have multiple security admin rules – and these rule collections target your network groups. This means the same rule collection can be applied to several virtual networks at once.

Security admin rules are similar to NSG rules, but security admin rules will be evaluated before NSG rules. If the security admin rule has an action type of “Deny” or “Always Allow,” traffic hitting that security admin rule will get blocked / delivered, and won’t hit a downstream NSG. Security admin rules also let you soft “Allow” traffic, so that other users like app team owners can handle the traffic as desired via NSGs.

💼 Use cases 
So, what can I do with AVNM’s security admin rules?
· 🧱Apply guardrail security rules, like blocking high-risk ports, on an entire organization to mitigate risks of misconfig and security holes. You can provide exceptions with higher-priority security admin rules as needed!
· 🔒Enforce standard security rules on all existing and new virtual networks without risk of change by non-admin users.
· ✅Ensure essential traffic, such as monitoring services and program updates, is not accidentally blocked.
· 0️⃣ Day-0 protection for newly provisioned network resources. Make this seamless by defining your network group with Azure Policy to automatically capture your new resource and have security admin rules applied.
· 👍Flexibly allow downstream teams to configure their NSGs as needed for more specific traffic dictation.

💡Where can I learn more?
🔗AVNM’s security admin GA update: https://lnkd.in/gZnqwM4D  
🔗Tech Community blog & walkthrough on AVNM security: https://lnkd.in/gcSUKXQD 
🔗AVNM public docs on security admin configs: https://lnkd.in/g2jkh3v3 

🚀Thanks for tuning in to AVNM Friday! Drop your Qs and feedback in the comments, and check back next week for more AVNM insights + tips to elevate your cloud game 🚀 

🟥🟩🟦🟨

☁️ Azure Virtual Network Manager (AVNM) Fridays: The Basics ☁️
I’m excited to dive into a series on a fantastic product I’ve been working on – Azure Virtual Network Manager. Every week, I’ll share a piece of AVNM’s magic – and today, we’ll start with the basics!

Whether you're a seasoned cloud pro or just starting your cloud journey, AVNM is a game-changer in managing your Azure network resources globally and at scale. Today, the AVNM service is generally available, along with its hub and spoke connectivity configuration and security admin configuration (in 30 regions and counting).

🤔 What is AVNM and how does it work?
Azure Virtual Network Manager is a centralized network management solution that helps you manage the connectivity and security of your network resources. Once you create your network manager resource, putting this into action only takes 3 main steps:
   1️⃣ Group your network resources however you see fit. You can even automatically group your resources by defining conditions.
   2️⃣ Create your connectivity and security admin configurations to achieve your desired topology and enforce organization-level policies.
   3️⃣ Deploy your configurations onto your network groups in any regions you want.

🔑 Key benefits
So, what makes AVNM stand out? 
   · 🌐Centralized controls --> simplified global management across regions and subscriptions.
   · ✨Automatic configuration application on your network resources --> connectivity maintenance and day-zero protection, even for new resources.
   · 🔐Enforced security rules across your organization’s new and existing network resources --> mitigate risk of changes or security holes.
   · 🌍Regional deployment of configuration changes --> safely test in regions, fix, & roll forward.

💡Where can I learn more?
🔗AVNM's most recent GA update: https://lnkd.in/gZnqwM4D
🔗AVNM public docs: https://lnkd.in/gsFHPEym
🔗Wired For Hybrid deep-dive: https://lnkd.in/gZQ2-9XF
🔗Azure blog on AVNM use cases: https://lnkd.in/g7V5_kpG
🔗AVNM product page: https://lnkd.in/gdFUFmx8

🚀Thanks for tuning in to AVNM Friday! Drop your Qs and feedback in the comments, and check back next week for more AVNM insights + tips to elevate your cloud game 🚀

🟥🟩🟦🟨

#AVNM #azure #AzureVirtualNetworkManager #cloud #cloudjourney  #networks #networksecurity #networksolutions #networkmanagement
